name.text: Week 14 – Q1: Media Classification and Skin Depth

questiontext.text:
Scenario: You work at a company that designs electromagnetic shielding for electronic equipment. A client needs to protect sensitive instruments from interference at \(f = {@f_MHz@}\) MHz. They are considering a {@thickness_mm@} mm layer of {@mat_name@} as shielding material.

Your task: characterize the electromagnetic properties of this material and determine whether the proposed shielding is effective.

The material properties are:

  • Conductivity: \(\sigma = {@sigma_c@} \times 10^{{@sigma_e@}}\) S/m
  • Relative permittivity: \(\varepsilon_r = {@eps_r@}\)
  • Relative permeability: \(\mu_r = {@mu_r@}\)

Part A — Material Characterization

(a) To determine whether conduction current or displacement current dominates in this material, compute the loss tangent \(\tan\delta = \dfrac{\sigma}{\omega\varepsilon}\).

\(\tan\delta = \) [[input:ans1]] [[validation:ans1]]

Enter a number, e.g., 5.67e3. Scientific notation is accepted.

(b) Based on your loss tangent, classify this medium:

[[input:ans2]] [[validation:ans2]]

Part B — Attenuation and Penetration

(c) Compute the attenuation constant \(\alpha\) (in Np/m) using the formula appropriate for your medium type:

  • Good conductor (\(\tan\delta \gg 1\)): \(\alpha = \sqrt{\pi f \mu \sigma}\)
  • Low-loss dielectric (\(\tan\delta \ll 1\)): \(\alpha \approx \dfrac{\sigma}{2}\sqrt{\dfrac{\mu}{\varepsilon}}\)

where \(\mu = \mu_r\mu_0\) and \(\varepsilon = \varepsilon_r\varepsilon_0\).

\(\alpha = \) [[input:ans3]] [[validation:ans3]] Np/m

Enter the value in Np/m, e.g., 12.3.

(d) The skin depth \(\delta_s\) is the distance over which the wave amplitude drops to \(1/e \approx 37\%\) of its surface value. Compute \(\delta_s = 1/\alpha\).

\(\delta_s = \) [[input:ans4]] [[validation:ans4]] m

Enter the value in metres, e.g., 0.234.

Part C — Shielding Evaluation

(e) If the client changes to a system operating at double the original frequency, how does \(\alpha\) change?

[[input:ans5]] [[validation:ans5]]

(f) The proposed shielding layer is {@thickness_mm@} mm thick. How many skin depths does this correspond to?

Number of skin depths = [[input:ans6]] [[validation:ans6]]

Enter a dimensionless number, e.g., 23.7.

(g) A shield is generally considered effective if its thickness is at least 5 skin depths (blocking more than 99% of incident wave power). Based on your result in (f), is the proposed {@thickness_mm@} mm layer of {@mat_name@} an effective shield at {@f_MHz@} MHz?

[[input:ans7]] [[validation:ans7]]

generalfeedback.text:
Worked Solution

Given: \(\sigma = {@sigma_c@} \times 10^{{@sigma_e@}}\) S/m, \(\varepsilon_r = {@eps_r@}\), \(\mu_r = {@mu_r@}\), \(f = {@f_MHz@}\) MHz.

(a) Loss tangent:

\[\omega = 2\pi \times {@f_MHz@} \times 10^6 \text{ rad/s}, \quad \varepsilon = {@eps_r@}\,\varepsilon_0\]

\[\tan\delta = \frac{\sigma}{\omega\varepsilon} = {@tans1@}\]

(b) Classification: {@if is_conductor then "Good conductor" else "Low-loss dielectric"@}.

{@if is_conductor then "Since tan delta >> 1, conduction current dominates — this is a good conductor." else "Since tan delta << 1, displacement current dominates — this is a low-loss dielectric."@}

(c) Attenuation constant:

{@if is_conductor then "

Using the good-conductor formula:

\\[\\alpha = \\sqrt{\\pi f \\mu \\sigma}\\]

" else "

Using the low-loss formula:

\\[\\alpha = \\frac{\\sigma}{2}\\sqrt{\\frac{\\mu}{\\varepsilon}} = \\frac{\\sigma\\,\\eta}{2}\\]

where \\(\\eta = \\sqrt{\\mu/\\varepsilon}\\) is the intrinsic impedance of the medium.

"@}

\[\alpha = {@tans3@} \text{ Np/m}\]

(d) Skin depth:

\[\delta_s = 1/\alpha = {@tans4@} \text{ m}\]

(e) Effect of doubling frequency:

{@if is_conductor then "

For a good conductor, \\(\\alpha = \\sqrt{\\pi f \\mu \\sigma} \\propto \\sqrt{f}\\). Doubling \\(f\\) increases \\(\\alpha\\) by \\(\\sqrt{2}\\).

" else "

For a low-loss dielectric, \\(\\alpha \\approx (\\sigma/2)\\sqrt{\\mu/\\varepsilon}\\). This expression does not depend on frequency (for non-dispersive media). So \\(\\alpha\\) does not change when \\(f\\) doubles.

"@}

This distinction is physically important: in conductors, higher frequencies are attenuated faster (skin effect); in low-loss dielectrics, attenuation is set by the material's conductivity and impedance, not frequency.

(f) Number of skin depths:

The proposed layer is {@thickness_mm@} mm = {@thickness_m@} m thick.

\[n = \frac{\text{thickness}}{\delta_s} = \frac{{@thickness_m@}}{{@tans4@}} = {@tans6@}\]

(g) Shielding effectiveness:

{@if is(shield_class = 1) then "The layer is well above 5 skin depths — it provides excellent shielding. Good conductors have extremely small skin depths (micrometres at MHz frequencies), so even thin metallic sheets are effective electromagnetic shields." else if is(shield_class = 2) then "The layer is far below 1 skin depth — it provides virtually no shielding. Low-loss dielectrics have very large skin depths (metres or more), so no practical thickness of this material can block the wave at this frequency." else "The layer is between 1 and 5 skin depths — it provides partial shielding but may not be sufficient for reliable protection."@}

This result illustrates a fundamental principle: metals shield effectively through absorption, while dielectrics do not. The classification in part (b) already predicted this outcome.

questionnote.text: {@sconcat("idx=", idx, " ", mat_name, " sigma=", sigma, " er=", eps_r, " mur=", mu_r, " f=", f_MHz, "MHz conductor=", is_conductor, " thick=", thickness_mm, "mm nskins=", n_skins)@}

falsefeedback.text: Incorrect. With \(\tan\delta = {@tans1@}\): if \(\gg 1\) it is a good conductor; if \(\ll 1\) it is a low-loss dielectric.

truefeedback.text: You used the wrong formula for this medium type. Check your classification in (b): {@if is_conductor then "this is a good conductor, use \\(\\alpha = \\sqrt{\\pi f \\mu \\sigma}\\)" else "this is a low-loss dielectric, use \\(\\alpha = (\\sigma/2)\\sqrt{\\mu/\\varepsilon}\\)"@}.
You used \(\mu_0\) instead of \(\mu = \mu_r\mu_0\). For this material \(\mu_r = {@mu_r@}\).

falsefeedback.text: Incorrect. {@if is_conductor then "Good conductor: \\(\\alpha = \\sqrt{\\pi f \\mu \\sigma}\\)" else "Low-loss: \\(\\alpha = (\\sigma/2)\\sqrt{\\mu/\\varepsilon}\\)"@} where \(\mu = \mu_r\mu_0\), \(\varepsilon = \varepsilon_r\varepsilon_0\).

truefeedback.text: Correct. {@if is_conductor then "For a good conductor, \\(\\alpha \\propto \\sqrt{f}\\), so doubling \\(f\\) increases \\(\\alpha\\) by \\(\\sqrt{2}\\)." else "For a low-loss dielectric, \\(\\alpha = (\\sigma/2)\\sqrt{\\mu/\\varepsilon}\\) — no frequency dependence. \\(\\alpha\\) does not change."@}

falsefeedback.text: Incorrect. Look at your formula from part (c): does it contain \(f\)? {@if is_conductor then "The good-conductor formula \\(\\alpha = \\sqrt{\\pi f \\mu \\sigma}\\) depends on \\(\\sqrt{f}\\)." else "The low-loss formula \\(\\alpha = (\\sigma/2)\\sqrt{\\mu/\\varepsilon}\\) has no \\(f\\) in it."@}

truefeedback.text: Correct. The layer is {@tans6@} skin depths thick.
Your method is correct (thickness divided by skin depth), but your skin depth from part (d) was not correct. Half credit for correct follow-through.
You forgot to convert the thickness from mm to m. The layer is {@thickness_mm@} mm = {@thickness_m@} m. Divide by \(\delta_s\) in metres to get the number of skin depths.

falsefeedback.text: Incorrect. Number of skin depths = thickness / \(\delta_s\). Convert the thickness to metres first: {@thickness_mm@} mm = {@thickness_m@} m. Then divide by your \(\delta_s\) from part (d).

truefeedback.text: Correct. {@if is(shield_class = 1) then "The layer is many skin depths thick — it provides excellent shielding against electromagnetic interference." else if is(shield_class = 2) then "The layer is a tiny fraction of one skin depth — this material provides virtually no shielding at this frequency." else "The layer is between 1 and 5 skin depths — partial shielding only."@}

falsefeedback.text: Incorrect. Compare your result in (f) to the 5 skin-depth threshold. {@if is(shield_class = 1) then "Your calculation shows the layer is many skin depths thick — this is an effective shield." else if is(shield_class = 2) then "Your calculation shows the layer is far less than 1 skin depth — this material cannot serve as an electromagnetic shield." else "Your calculation shows the layer is between 1 and 5 skin depths."@}

hint.text:
Where to start: Before computing anything about attenuation or shielding, you need to know what kind of material you are dealing with. The loss tangent tells you whether conduction current or displacement current dominates.

A large \(\tan\delta\) (much greater than 1) means free charges move easily — the material behaves like a metal. A small \(\tan\delta\) (much less than 1) means bound charges dominate — the material behaves like an insulator.

Compute \(\tan\delta = \sigma/(\omega\varepsilon)\) where \(\omega = 2\pi f\) and \(\varepsilon = \varepsilon_r\varepsilon_0\). Then use this value to choose the correct attenuation formula.

Material properties matter: When computing \(\alpha\), make sure you use the full material parameters: \(\mu = \mu_r\mu_0\) and \(\varepsilon = \varepsilon_r\varepsilon_0\).

Most materials are non-magnetic (\(\mu_r = 1\)), but ferromagnetic materials like iron can have \(\mu_r \gg 1\). Forgetting \(\mu_r\) for a magnetic material will give a significantly wrong attenuation constant — and therefore a wrong skin depth and shielding assessment.

Frequency dependence and shielding: Look at the structure of your attenuation formula — does it contain the frequency \(f\)? In one type of medium, \(\alpha \propto \sqrt{f}\), so doubling the frequency increases attenuation by \(\sqrt{2}\). In the other type, \(\alpha\) has no frequency dependence at all.

For parts (f) and (g): the number of skin depths equals the layer thickness divided by \(\delta_s\). Remember to convert mm to m. A layer of 5 or more skin depths blocks over 99% of the wave power.
